---
title: "Washington Highway CMF Analysis"
subtitle: "Benchmark vs Best Model"
author: "MetaCount Research Group"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    self-contained: true
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
execute:
  enabled: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root { --teal:#0a6c74; --orange:#d96f32; --ink:#152238; --paper:#f8f6f2; }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2 { color: var(--teal); }
.reveal h3 { color: var(--orange); }
.reveal section { font-size: 0.88rem; line-height: 1.35; }
table { font-size: 0.78rem; width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; }
th { background: var(--teal); color: #fff; padding: 5px 8px; text-align: left; }
td { padding: 4px 8px; }
tr:nth-child(even) td { background: #eef6f7; }
.co { border-left: 4px solid var(--orange); background: #fff8f3; padding: 6px 10px; }
</style>
```

## Objective

Show one thing clearly: does the new hierarchical model beat the benchmark model on out-of-sample performance, and what do the coefficient differences imply for safety interpretation.

---

## Dataset Snapshot

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Segments | 275 |
| Response | FREQ (crash count) |
| Main exposure | AADT |
| Offset | log(Length) |
| Family used in selected model | NB2 |

---

## Benchmark Model

$$
\log(\hat{\mu}) = \alpha + \log(\text{AADT}) + \log(L) + \sum_j \beta_j x_j
$$

| Baseline characteristics | Value |
|---|---|
| AADT treatment | Fixed offset style |
| AADT-varying CMFs | No |
| Random heterogeneity | No |

---

## Best Model Found

$$
\log(\hat{\mu}) = \alpha + \beta_{\text{AADT}}\log(A) + \sum_j \beta_j x_j + \sum_k \gamma_k x_k\log(A) + \log(L)
$$

| Best-model characteristics | Value |
|---|---|
| AADT treatment | Estimated elasticity |
| AADT-varying CMFs | Yes |
| Random heterogeneity support | Yes (when RP refit available) |

---

## Search Outcome (Concise)

::: {.content-visible when-format="pptx"}
![Search convergence (selection-stage metrics).](../results/ex16_3_cmf/search_convergence.png)
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="revealjs"}
<iframe src="../results/ex16_3_cmf/search_convergence.html" width="100%" height="500px" title="Convergence"></iframe>
:::

{{< include ../results/ex16_3_cmf/search_phase_differences.md >}}

---

## Benchmark vs Best: Key Result

{{< include ../results/ex16_3_cmf/benchmark_comparison.md >}}

<div class="co">
Lower BIC, lower Test RMSE, and higher Test R2 all point in the same direction: the best model outperforms the benchmark on held-out data.
</div>

---

## Observed vs Predicted Comparison

::: {.content-visible when-format="pptx"}
![Observed and predicted crashes for benchmark vs best model.](../results/ex16_3_cmf/model_comparison.png)
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="revealjs"}
<iframe src="../results/ex16_3_cmf/model_comparison.html" width="100%" height="500px" title="Model comparison"></iframe>
:::

---

## Difference Plot: Best Minus Benchmark

![Prediction delta by traffic level: hierarchical minus benchmark.](../results/ex16_3_cmf/model_prediction_delta.png)

Positive values mean the best model predicts more crashes than benchmark at that traffic level; negative values mean fewer.

---

## What Coefficient Differences Suggest About Safety

{{< include ../results/ex16_3_cmf/safety_implications.md >}}

---

## Final Takeaway

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Did the best model beat benchmark on held-out data? | Yes |
| Is the improvement consistent across metrics? | Yes (BIC, RMSE, R2) |
| Does the model change safety interpretation? | Yes, via coefficient direction/magnitude differences and interaction effects |

Next action: use the safety-implication terms to prioritize policy checks on road segments where benchmark and best-model predictions diverge most.

---

## Appendix: Compact Coefficients

{{< include ../results/ex16_3_cmf/literature_vs_proposed_coefficients_pptx.md >}}
